speaker: interviewer
text: Take number six, interview with Mr Zoltan Wieder. You were talking about your father having gone back to Dachau and that there were wagons going back and forth. How far-- it's 10 kilometers to--

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 kilometers, about 10, 12 kilometers. And the third or the fourth day after he went back, he sent me a note with this man-- which I didn't know at that time-- with this man with a wagon, who went with the dads-- who took the dads to Dachau. I don't know he found him there how. He sent me a note, he's fine. They checked him out. Because the doctor there told me he had pneumonia. And he sent notes back to me. He has no pneumonia, but he has a very bad cold, and he's much better. But we couldn't get him-- bring him back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was there and this man, I was able to send with him-- after, you know, I was working in the warehouse and going for bread. So I was really able to send him a bread, every week a bread or some bread and some margarine and some jell-o And write him a letter, which was unheard of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And able to get back answer from him, he got it, and he's OK. And we kept on this-- because this was sometime in February '45. We kept in on through April, about two, three, weeks-- two weeks before the liberation. Then it stopped, everything. They didn't send this guy anymore to Dachau. And I didn't hear from him. And I was sure he was alive. I was sure he was going to be liberated there. And-- and then I find out what's happened after the liberation, which we'll go back to that when we talk about--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Allach?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Allach I was actually since August through the liberation, which was April 27, I think, '45. So about nine months, 10 months I was in Allach, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become aware that the war conditions were changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I became aware in February or March. [BACKGROUND NOISE] When the Americans were bombing around Munich, they were bombing Munich. And they were bombing the-- because we had near the camp some German army units with anti-aircraft batteries. And at that time, one day-- when they started bombing, the SS, they went to bomb bunkers right away. And we were looking-- especially me from the kitchen, I went outside and I was looking to the planes. And the planes dropped leaflets. And one leaflet fell near the kitchen. And I was looking around, I didn't see any SS around there, so I pulled in the leaflet. And I saw on the leaflet-- I saw on the leaflet American troops marched in [GERMAN] Leipzig. American allied troops, allied. So the allied forces are marching through Leipzig. And then we knew they are already in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember liberation day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember-- I remember very vividly. About one week before the liberation, about one week before the liberation, we heard one day-- we looked out, we didn't see the-- the-- the SS troops. They run away from the towers. We had towers around the camp. And we didn't know what's happened. And there in the kitchen and in the warehouse, there were Polish prisoners, not Jews. They were also in the camp there. One was the kitchen chief. He was a Czechoslovakian, in and the Polish guy was from Warsaw. And he was the-- he was the chief of the-- of the warehouse. And they-- I don't know. He went out when the troops-- when the SS run away, he went out to their barracks. Because they had the barracks outside the camp. And he went out. He brought in a radio. And he brought in a radio, and he put on the radio. And then we heard they was approaching Munich. And they-- they-- they wanted to-- they was-- they were asking the people not to fight anymore, the army not to fight and so on. A then we knew that's why they run away, the SS troops. And then what's happened, we organized ourselves. We put outside white flags. And we put some of our people in the towers there. And then in another two days, the SS came back. And they came back again, so it looks like that they were fighting again. And it was-- they went through another two or three days. And then one night, it started at about-- I think it was April 26th. One day, April 26th, one day before the liberation. No, actually it was two days. Came in-- the camp opened the doors of the camp, and came in a woman from another camp. They brought them-- because what they did, before the liberation, they tried to-- because the order from Himmler was to kill everybody. And they just didn't have time. And they brought in a prisoners-- I mean, from our people, women.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans brought in prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought them in. And they brought them in to our camp. And they took them-- to the back of our camp was an empty camp. And they put them there. And we were looking-- I was at that time in the kitchen, I went out to look who's coming in. Maybe I see somebody. In the first line, I see my sister-in-law, which wasn't at that time my sister-in-law. She was my best-- she was my younger sister's best friend. Her mother, her aunt, two other girls and two other women from my town, which I know very good. And they-- they-- they-- they brought them into our camp. And they took them to near our camp, to the women's camp. And they came from someplace from the west, west Germany from someplace, as the-- as the German troops were withdrawing, so they brought them. And they put them in the camp. And I remember that evening I was able to go over to their camp and go under the fence and took them some food from the kitchen, from our kitchen. I took over some food to them. And so we thought we were going to be liberated soon. And after I gave them the food, I was trying to get back to the kitchen. And they were starting shouting. The tanks, American tanks, were-- were fighting against the German tanks. They were near our camp, because I told you there were the-- the-- the anti-- batteries, anti-- and there were about-- the whole went through our camp. They were shooting with the tanks. And at that time, I tried to-- I remembered one doctor from my town, from my wife's town, he was comforting the sick. And there were some sick people there inside. And he was hit by shrapnel. He died there. And there were terrible fighting there, right over our heads. And I was staying outside the-- the kitchen. And near me was I remember a French, somebody from France, a Jew also. So we were talking to each other that I hope in the morning we are going to be liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you able to get back to the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was able to get back to the kitchen. But on the-- on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground. I went back to the kitchen. And finally, I am looking, he's dead. So shrapnel hit him right near me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I run into the kitchen then. And I-- and I-- we were on the ground, right away we lay down under the big, big-- where we were cooking-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --big stoves there. And we were laying down. And the whole night was fighting. And in the morning, about 7 o'clock, the first American troops came in. It was such a-- such-- when-- when they came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you see first?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Few tanks. Because I tell you, we never imagined we will be-- we always said they are going to kill us. It's no way we are going to escape. We always said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you see first? What was the first thing you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw-- first I saw an American soldier, with a-- with a-- with a tank coming in, getting through the wire doors, the wire fences and coming in. And-- and a few soldiers, and they came out right away from the tanks. And we were going there-- I mean, whoever was able to walk. Because hundreds and hundreds, they were near dead. They couldn't walk. They just thought about it. And I was in better condition, because I worked there in the kitchen. So I was able to walk. And people were going crazy. And then they brought in some beds-- camps with-- with-- with they opened, they made up like military beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took-- that was the first thing. They took away the clothing from us, and they burned everything. And then they gave us some military clothing, German, from the German warehouses, some German military clothing. And-- and finally we were liberated. That was something unbelievable. And these people from my town, they were liberated too. And my future sister-in-law, her mother, and other girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't know what to do with ourselves. Could you talk to any of the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the American troops? Yeah, we were talking to the-- to some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were talking some-- we were trying to talk some English, but we didn't know, you know, something here. But everyone could-- some, they spoke a little German and a little bit. And they were kissing them. And it was just unbelievable. And they told us we have to get-- we have to get out. They didn't let me out from the kitchen. We have to stay there for another three weeks to cook for the people. And whoever worked in the kitchen was staying there in the kitchen for another three weeks. And we went in to get some supplies in the German warehouses. And they brought in all kinds of supplies and all kind of meat and other things. And people were not used with any fat, and a lot of people died. They got diarrhea, and then they stopped it. But in the beginning, a day or two, they got this food. And you know, they were not used to eat any fat in anything for-- for months and months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you connect with the rest of your family? How did you find out what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, we were about-- I was hoping my father is alive. So as soon as they let me, I went into Dachau to look for him. I went into Dachau, and-- and I was looking-- I went to the American commander. And I was looking all around there. And they told me there-- they took him out, and they took him to the south, Innsbruck area. Because the orders were to kill. They took them on the roads, and they tried to kill them wherever they could. And they said they took a lot of-- thousands and thousands of people to the south.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I went and I-- and I was terribly-- I didn't know nothing about my mother and two sisters at that time. They were trying-- they were starting talking to people, they were trying talking to people. And-- and [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] they were trying talking to people and-- and-- and what's happened. And there were crematoriums and there were killings, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we still-- we still were thinking maybe-- I was still thinking maybe they escaped some way. Maybe they-- they did and-- But in the meantime, I was looking for my father. Because that was my first goal now, to find him. And I was running around in camps, different camps, in Innsbruck area. And finally in one camp, one guy comes to me and he says, look, I know your father. You look like him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said-- I said to him, I'm looking for him. Where is he? He said he was staying back in-- in-- in-- in Dachau. They didn't bring with us about 3,000 some people left there. Go back there, I'm sure you will find him. So I went back again with trains and this and that. And I went back to Dachau. And I went to the American commander. And he was calling the hospital staff that he was in the register, in everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took him about three hours. And-- and I'm sitting in his office, in front of him. And I hear somebody, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought he found him, I was thinking to myself. And at the time, they were telling him he was killed. He was poisoned three days-- [SOB] before the liberation. How did you find your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I just want to tell that was such a shock for me. I was crying for weeks and weeks. Because I was sure he will be-- he's alive, he will be liberated. My brother, I heard about my brother in when I was in Bergen-Belsen. I heard-- somebody told me he escaped the labor camp, and he is in Romania. Then I decided to go back to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit. How did you get to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Feldafing, and-- and which was a camp there. They took us there for recreation and to build us up a little bit, give us better food and build us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feldafing was a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a-- It was like a DP-- they called it a sanatorium at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who ran it? The-- the the Germans ran it, actually. They had-- that was before a recreation camp for the SS, like a sanatorium type. And they had nurses there, and they had kitchens there. I mean chefs there in the kitchen. And they kept these people there to work-- and to work, to cook for the Jews there. And the nurses, they'd go to-- to see to the sick and give medicine. But it was an American commander. Actually, it was a Jewish guy. I don't recall his name. He was the commander of this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Feldafing considered a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A DP camp, yeah. It was considered a DP camp. And he was the commander of the camp. There were American troops there, and he was the commander of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us by train from Allach to Feldafing, which was about three, four hours ride by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a place you chose to go? Or did they-- did you have any choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have any choice. They took us there. Because I was actually a child, I was a young man. I was 18, 19 years old. And I was alone on the world. And-- [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us there. And they put us in that camp there. And-- and it was that American commander. He was, I think, a colonel in the-- in the military.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there they gave us food, naturally, were there food and medicines. And we were there in the camp. And I was still-- I had some friends there who liberated. I had a good friend there, who I knew from before. He wasn't from my town. And we were together there. And we were there. And in the meantime, I was running around, looking for my father. And then I find out about him. And then one day, I find out my uncle, who is-- who died now. He lived in Israel after the war. And I find out about him. He's-- he was liberated, and he's alive in St. Ottilien, which was a monastery type-- it was a hospital at that time. There were monks there and-- and-- and nurses there. And he was there in that camp. It was actually a hospital. He was liberated there, because he was sick. So he was there, they were treating him. I think he had typhus before the liberation. And when I went to him, he was already-- when I saw him, I went to him, and I told him, you come with me now. I'm taking you to Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him from there to Feldafing. And then we find out his two daughters escaped in Bergen-Belsen. Lily and-- and Blima, which I talked to before. She was the [INAUDIBLE] Blima, the big Blima.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they escaped there. And then Lily, who was speaking some English. She was an interpreter. She told them her father is in Feldafing. And the British-- there was a British zone there-- they gave them a military vehicle with a-- with a driver to come down to Feldafing and take her father. And then we went all together, myself and I had two friends who came with me also, from my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated in Feldafing. What was their name?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bergen-Belsen was a DP camp also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at that time. But that was a death camp actually, Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the DP camp? [INTERPOSING VOICES] Who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British.

speaker: interviewer
text: The British.

speaker: interviewee
text: The British were in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish presence in the camp too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were all Jewish there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I meant in the leadership or the running of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: The leadership-- Yeah, I think there were some Jews who were-- I don't remember them. I don't remember names or something. But I know-- we were-- there was two girls from my town. Actually, they were not from my town, from Satu Mare. And they were nephews of one of my father's friend who was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I meant was there a Jewish organizational presence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Organizational-- I don't recall. But I know they were-- they were-- they were talking about that time too. They will try to take us to Palestine. And I remember the American commander in Feldafing, he said, don't go back to-- to-- to Romania. Try to stay here, and maybe we'll get you out from here. But we didn't know about America at that time, we can come. Or even to Palestine, it was-- we didn't know they will be able to go or something at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen I was there about eight weeks-- six to eight weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't work there, no. So we were in the camp. And we got some food. Plus we got some better food, because that girl was working in the British kitchen. And she brought us some supplies from there, some conserves and whatever she could. Yeah, we got the regular food, what they gave us there. Naturally better than we had before. And we were there. And some of them, they were trying at the time to organize to go to Palestine or to go to America. And but we didn't know at that time what's-- and when I find out my brother is alive--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find out, somebody came from Romania back at that time to Germany. He was trying to get to America. And he was telling me, look, your brother is alive. He is in Romania. He escaped the labor camp, and the Russians liberated him. And then I decided-- because until then, I was thinking not to go back. But deep in my heart, I was always thinking maybe I will find somebody from my sister, from my mother. Even they told me it was crematoriums and this. But deep in my heart, I said maybe. Maybe they did escape some way. Maybe I find somebody. And then I decided to go back. Besides, I find out about my father-- my brother. Maybe if my brother would not be there, I would never have gone back. But thank God he was there. So I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Romania, at that time we went with some-- they took us with some trains and very bad transportation, about three weeks until we got to Budapest. And my brother was-- he heard about the people are coming back from the camps. And he found out about myself, I'm coming back with a train. And he was waiting every day, after the trains there, when they come in, if somebody comes from the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was there at the train station, rail station, when I came in. So then we went-- actually, we find out later. Because we went first to the city, to the joint distribution, there in the city, in Budapest. We didn't have money, we didn't have nothing. We went in the tramways, you know, the tramways there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the conductor came, and he wanted money. We said we don't have money. We come from the concentration camps. So he let us and now he said, no, I want the money. And then a guy says, lady-- she was a conductor-- hold it. I will pay for them. So he paid, and he says, today [HEBREW]. Today is the last day of Sukkot. So I find out he is a Jew. And he told me-- he was from Budapest-- he told me I will show you where the joint distribution is. They have a kitchen there. You can get some food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to that kitchen. And we got some food. And then my brother-- yeah, at that time he didn't know. The next day he was looking for me, for another train, didn't find me. So finally he found me, because we went back to the station. So in the afternoon he found me at the rail station. And then he said I'm here now. And I had two cousins who were liberated. They were from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were liberated, and they were living at that time in Budapest. And they had one room there. So we went there. And we were sleeping on the floor there two nights. And the third day, we went back with a Russian truck. My brother was there with a military truck, because there were no trains, regular trains, at that time. And we went back to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Baia-Sprie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There in Romania--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Baia-Sprie.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baia-Sprie we found that 300 people who were living there before, there were left only about 15 or 16 people. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my house was still there. And my brother was living in that house at that time, because he was there a few months before. And so he was living there, and a few other friends, younger people who escaped. And in the beginning we were cooking together there. Then we had a woman there who cooked for us there. And then later on, my-- when I went there actually, when I went home, my brother and two other friends, they had a store. They opened a store. And they were trying to make a living there with that store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I arrived, I had already my brother was there. He had a little money, so-- Were you able to find some of the things that you had left there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found some of the linen that the neighbor, they gave us back some of this linen and some dishes we left there. No clothing because we didn't have anything else. And we found that little jewelry what my mother had, my father-- and actually my wife had that. I gave to my wife that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Earring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not earring, I gave to my daughter. And the ring I gave to my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to change our tape again.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
